question: Which of the following is an example of a finite set?
options: The set of all natural numbers less than 10
The set of all points on a line
The set of all prime numbers
The set of all integers
explanation: A finite set has a countable number of elements. The set {1, 2, 3, ..., 9} has exactly 9 elements.

question: Which of these sets is infinite?
options: The set of vowels in the English alphabet
The set of days in a week
The set of solutions to the equation x + 5 = 10
The set of all multiples of 5
explanation: An infinite set has an uncountable number of elements. The set {5, 10, 15, 20, ...} continues without end.

question: What is the cardinality of the power set of a finite set with 'n' elements?
options: n
n²
2n
2ⁿ
explanation: The power set (set of all subsets) of a finite set with n elements has cardinality 2ⁿ, as each element can either be included or not included in a subset.

question: According to set theory, how can an infinite set be characterized?
options: It has a very large number of elements
It can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with a proper subset of itself
Its elements cannot be listed
It has no empty subsets
explanation: A fundamental property of infinite sets (Dedekind-infinite) is that they have the same cardinality as some proper subset. For example, the set of natural numbers can be mapped to the set of even numbers.

question: Consider the set S = {x ∈ ℝ : 0 < x < 1}. What is the cardinality of this set compared to the set of all natural numbers ℕ?
options: S is finite, so its cardinality is undefined compared to ℕ
explanation: The set (0, 1) is uncountably infinite (its cardinality is the continuum c), while the set of natural numbers is countably infinite (ℵ₀). It is proven that c > ℵ₀.
